revision.comment: ページ [[玉垣]] を [[玉垣 (曖昧さ回避)]] へ移動
revision.text: #REDIRECT [[玉垣 (曖昧さ回避)]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・神域の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。
樹木をめぐらせる柴垣が最も古い形式であると考えられる。形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には寄進者の名前が刻まれることがある。
玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを端垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと称するが、これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。[[伊勢神宮]]内宮では四重に垣がめぐらされているが、一番内側のものを端垣、中の2つを玉垣、一番外側を板垣という。
[[Category:神社|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・神域の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。

玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを端垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと称するが、これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。[[伊勢神宮]]内宮では四重に垣がめぐらされているが、一番内側のものを端垣、中の2つを玉垣、一番外側を板垣という。
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・神域の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。
樹木をめぐらせる柴垣が最も古い形式であると考えられる。形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には寄進者の名前が刻まれることがある。
玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを端垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと称するが、これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。[[伊勢神宮]]内宮では四重に垣がめぐらされているが、一番内側のものを端垣、中の2つを玉垣、一番外側を板垣という。
<gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・神域の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。
樹木をめぐらせる柴垣が最も古い形式であると考えられる。形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には寄進者の名前が刻まれることがある。
== 名称 == ;同じ意味の言葉 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「神聖な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。 ;複数の囲いの場合 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを端垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと呼ばれる。これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。 :[[伊勢神宮]]内宮の御正殿（ごしょうでん）の周囲には四重に垣がめぐらされているが、内側から順に、端垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。
<gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・神域の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。
樹木をめぐらせる柴垣が最も古い形式であると考えられる。形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には寄進者の名前が刻まれることがある。

<gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・[[神域]]の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。
== 概要 == === 由来と変遷 === 神道の始まりとされる[[古神道]]では、[[神 (神道)|神]]が鎮座する、隠れ住む[[山]]や[[森]]として、[[神奈備]]（かんなび・かむなび・かみなび）という神域があり、いわゆる[[鎮守の森]]や[[霊峰]]として崇められる山々のことである。これらが、木々の垣根として神域を守る役目を持つものを[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）と呼び、山の一部として取り出し岩垣としたものが[[磐座]]（いわくら）・磐境（いわさか）であり、同様に神域を守る[[結界]]とし[[注連縄]]を巻いた。
[[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。
=== 形態の変遷 === [[樹木]]をめぐらせる[[柴垣]]が最も古い形式であると考えられる。
形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。
材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には[[寄進]]した者の名前が刻まれることがある。
=== 名称と意味 === 現在の神社神道における玉垣の在り方と意味と名称 ;意味と同義語 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「[[神聖]]な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。
;重複の囲いの場合の意味と同義語 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを瑞垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと呼ばれる。これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。 :[[伊勢神宮]]内宮の御正殿（ごしょうでん）の周囲には四重に垣がめぐらされているが、内側から順に、瑞垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。
<gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}}
'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・[[神域]]の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。 <gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>
== 概要 == === 由来と変遷 === 神道の始まりとされる[[古神道]]では、[[神 (神道)|神]]が鎮座する、隠れ住む[[山]]や[[森]]として、[[神奈備]]（かんなび・かむなび・かみなび）という神域があり、いわゆる[[鎮守の森]]や[[霊峰]]として崇められる山々のことである。これらが、木々の垣根として神域を守る役目を持つものを[[神籬]]（ひもろぎ）と呼び、山の一部として取り出し岩垣としたものが[[磐座]]（いわくら）・磐境（いわさか）であり、同様に神域を守る[[結界]]とし[[注連縄]]を巻いた。
[[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。
=== 形態の変遷 === [[樹木]]をめぐらせる[[柴垣]]が最も古い形式であると考えられる。
形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。
材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には[[寄進]]した者の名前が刻まれることがある。
=== 名称と意味 === 現在の神社神道における玉垣の在り方と意味と名称 ;意味と同義語 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「[[神聖]]な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。
;重複の囲いの場合の意味と同義語 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを瑞垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと呼ばれる。これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。 :[[伊勢神宮]]内宮の御正殿（ごしょうでん）の周囲には四重に垣がめぐらされているが、内側から順に、瑞垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。
== 関連事項 == *[[神奈備]] *[[神籬]] *[[磐座]] *[[鎮守の森]]

[[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。

'''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・[[神域]]の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。 <gallery> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery>

;重複の囲いの場合の意味と同義語 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、一般には一番内側のものを瑞垣（みずがき）、それ以外のものを玉垣、あるいは荒垣、板垣などと呼ばれる。これらの名称が混用されている場合もある。 :[[伊勢神宮]]内宮の御正殿（ごしょうでん）の周囲には四重に垣がめぐらされているが、内側から順に、瑞垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。

== 概要 == === 由来と変遷 === [[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。

形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。
材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には[[寄進]]した者の名前が刻まれることがある。
=== 名称と意味 === 現在の神社神道における玉垣の在り方と意味と名称 ;意味と同義語 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。垣の内側を「垣内（かきつ）」、垣の外側を「垣外（かいと）」という。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「[[神聖]]な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。
;重複の囲いの場合の意味と同義語 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、その場合、名称で区別をつけるが、神社によっては内側の垣を瑞垣、外側の垣を板垣（いたがき）といったり、一番外側のものを荒垣（あらがき）または外垣といったり、その他、中垣（なかがき）、内垣など、様々な例がある。これらの名称が混用されている場合もあるが、概ね一番内側の垣を「瑞垣」とよぶことは一致している。[[伊勢神宮]]を例に挙げると、四重の垣がめぐらされ内側から順に、瑞垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。
== 神具 == 神具としての玉垣は、[[神棚]]の宮形を囲う形で配置される神社の玉垣の形状を模した器具である。
== 関連事項 == *[[神奈備]] *[[神籬]] *[[磐座]] *[[鎮守の森]]
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]] [[Category:神社|たまかき]] [[Category:神道|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}} <gallery style="float:right" widths="150px"> Image:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|本殿を囲む玉垣 Image:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|境内を囲む玉垣 </gallery> '''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・[[神域]]の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。'''瑞垣'''（みずがき）ともいう。
== 概要 == === 由来と変遷 === [[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。
=== 形態の変遷 === [[樹木]]をめぐらせる[[柴垣]]が最も古い形式であると考えられる。
形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。
材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には[[寄進]]した者の名前が刻まれることがある。
=== 名称と意味 === 現在の神社神道における玉垣の在り方と意味と名称 ;意味と同義語 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。垣の内側を「垣内（かきつ）」、垣の外側を「垣外（かいと）」という。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「[[神聖]]な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。

== 神具 == 神具としての玉垣は、[[神棚]]の宮形を囲う形で配置される神社の玉垣の形状を模した器具である。
== 関連事項 == *[[神奈備]] *[[神籬]] *[[磐座]] *[[鎮守の森]]
[[Category:神社建築|たまかき]] [[Category:神道|たまかき]]

{{Otheruses|神社の周囲の垣}} {{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:57 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月28日 (月) 07:57 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|本殿を囲む玉垣]] [[ファイル:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|境内を囲む玉垣]] '''玉垣'''（たまがき）とは、[[神社]]・[[神域]]の周囲にめぐらされる[[垣]]のことである。'''瑞垣'''（みずがき）ともいう。
== 概要 == === 由来と変遷 === [[神社神道]]が確立され、[[社]]や[[拝殿]]や[[本殿]]が、建立され敷地（自然との境界）が明確になるにつれ、曖昧であった[[常世]]と[[現世]]の境界でもある神域がはっきりと区別されるようになり、神籬と磐座・磐境が結びつき、石造の垣根などに代わり、現在の神社にみられる玉垣に変わっていった。
=== 形態の変遷 === [[樹木]]をめぐらせる[[柴垣]]が最も古い形式であると考えられる。形状は、厚板を並べた板玉垣、皮がついたままの木を用いた黒木玉垣、広く間を開ける透垣などがある。材質は木や石、近年ではコンクリートによるものもある。玉垣には[[寄進]]した者の名前が刻まれることがある。
== 名称 == ;意味と同義語 :瑞垣（みずがき）、斎垣（いみがき、いかき、いがき）、神垣（かみがき）は玉垣と同様にすべて同じ意味を持つ。垣の内側を「垣内（かきつ）」、垣の外側を「垣外（かいと）」という。 :「玉」や「瑞」、「斎」という言葉はともに「[[神聖]]な」「美しい」という意味を持ち、囲いの意味である「垣」と組み合わさって「神聖なすばらしい神の為の囲い」という意味の言葉となっている。 ;重複の囲いの場合の意味と同義語 :玉垣は複数めぐらすことがあり、その場合、名称で区別をつけるが、神社によっては内側の垣を瑞垣、外側の垣を板垣（いたがき）といったり、一番外側のものを荒垣（あらがき）または外垣といったり、その他、中垣（なかがき）、内垣など、様々な例がある。これらの名称が混用されている場合もあるが、概ね一番内側の垣を「瑞垣」とよぶことは一致している。[[伊勢神宮]]を例に挙げると、四重の垣がめぐらされ内側から順に、瑞垣、内玉垣（うちたまがき）、外玉垣（そとたまがき）、板垣と呼んでいる。
== 神具 == 神具としての玉垣は、[[神棚]]の宮形を囲う形で配置される神社の玉垣の形状を模した器具である。
== 脚注 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[神奈備]] *[[神籬]] *[[磐座]] *[[鎮守の森]]
{{日本の建築}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たまかき}} [[Category:神社建築]] [[Category:神道]] [[Category:囲い]]

It is also k..."
revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - **Ita-tamagaki**: Fences made from thick wooden planks. - **Kuroki-tamagaki**: Made from wood with the bark still attached. - **Sukigaki**: Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including **Mizugaki** (瑞垣), **Imigaki** (斎垣), and **Kamigaki** (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as **Itagaki** (板垣) for the outermost fence or **Aragaki** (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}
{{Architecture of Japan}}
[[Category:Shinto shrine architecture]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Fences]] [[ja:玉垣]]

Various styles have emerged, including: - **Ita-tamagaki**: Fences made from thick wooden planks. - **Kuroki-tamagaki**: Made from wood with the bark still attached. - **Sukigaki**: Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including **Mizugaki** (瑞垣), **Imigaki** (斎垣), and **Kamigaki** (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as **Itagaki** (板垣) for the outermost fence or **Aragaki** (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - **Ita-tamagaki**: Fences made from thick wooden planks. - **Kuroki-tamagaki**: Made from wood with the bark still attached. - **Sukigaki**: Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including **Mizugaki** (瑞垣), **Imigaki** (斎垣), and **Kamigaki** (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as **Itagaki** (板垣) for the outermost fence or **Aragaki** (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. - '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. - '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. - '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. - '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). ; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.

== Notes ==

== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. - '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. - '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}
{{Architecture of Japan}}

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).

{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: - '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. - '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. - '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes ==
== See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}
{{Architecture of Japan}}

=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森}}

revision.text: #redirect[[en:玉垣]] <includeonly></includeonly> <noinclude></noinclude> [[Category:automatic wikipedia redirects 玉]]

{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shinto shrine practices|ja|神社神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|haiden|ja|拝殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|honden|ja|本殿|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Utsushiyo|ja|現世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and rocks ({{ill|iwa|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|kamidana|ja|神棚|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|ja|神奈備|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Himorogi|ja|神籬|1=Himorogi|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Iwakura|ja|磐座|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Chinju-no-mori|ja|鎮守の森|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
{{Architecture of Japan}}
{{translated page|ja|玉垣|version=92880766|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|qq=}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As [[:en:Shrine Shinto|Shinto shrine practices]] became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|qq=}}, [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], and [[:en:Honden|honden]] were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|qq=}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|qq=}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|qq=}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As [[:en:Shrine Shinto|Shinto shrine practices]] became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], and [[:en:Honden|honden]] were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|qq=}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).

This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|qq=|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.

For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3078312]] Wikidata name: tamagaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|1=shin'iki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588853}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As [[:en:Shrine Shinto|Shinto shrine practices]] became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], and [[:en:Honden|honden]] were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3078312]] Wikidata name: tamagaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|1=shin'iki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588853}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As [[:en:Shrine Shinto|Shinto shrine practices]] became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], and [[:en:Honden|honden]] were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3078312]] Wikidata name: tamagaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|1=shin'iki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588853}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As [[:en:Shrine Shinto|Shinto shrine practices]] became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, [[:en:Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]], and [[:en:Honden|honden]] were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ([[:en:Present life|Utsushiyo]]) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ([[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]]) and rocks ([[:en:Iwakura rock|iwa]] or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate [[:en:Kamidana|kamidana]], mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * [[:en:Kannabi|Kannabi]] * [[:en:Himorogi|Himorogi]] * [[:en:Iwakura rock|Iwakura]] * [[:en:Chinju no Mori|Chinju-no-mori]]

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).

Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3078312]] Wikidata name: tamagaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:59, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|1=shin'iki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588853}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shrine Shinto|en|Shrine Shinto|WD=Q1527146|lt=Shinto shrine practices}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=haiden}}, and {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=honden}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Present life|en|Present life|WD=Q20183021|lt=Utsushiyo}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwa}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|Kamidana|en|Kamidana|WD=Q327584|lt=kamidana}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|en|Kannabi|WD=Q11588874|lt=Kannabi}} * {{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura}} * {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju-no-mori}}
{{Architecture of Japan}}
{{translated page|ja|玉垣|version=92880766|comment=Imported full ja history}}

== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shrine Shinto|en|Shrine Shinto|WD=Q1527146|lt=Shinto shrine practices}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=haiden}}, and {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=honden}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Present life|en|Present life|WD=Q20183021|lt=Utsushiyo}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwa}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=iwakura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|Kamidana|en|Kamidana|WD=Q327584|lt=kamidana}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|en|Kannabi|WD=Q11588874|lt=Kannabi}} * {{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura}} * {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju-no-mori}}

It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).

Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.

The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.

; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|Kamidana|en|Kamidana|WD=Q327584|lt=kamidana|1=Kamidana}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|en|Kannabi|WD=Q11588874|lt=Kannabi|1=Kannabi}} * {{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura|1=Iwakura rock}} * {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju-no-mori|1=Chinju no Mori}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tama-gaki (Shinto)]][[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tamagaki]][[es:Tamagaki]][[fi:Tamagaki]][[fr:Tamagaki]][[ja:玉垣]]
{{Otheruses|Perimeter fences around shrines}} {{Multiple issues|Uncited=August 28, 2017|Original research=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Owari_Oomiwa_Shrine_honden.jpeg|thumb|right|267x267px|Tamagaki surrounding the main shrine hall]] [[File:Tachibanajinjya_06c0859x.jpg|thumb|right|200x200px|Tamagaki surrounding the precincts]] '''Tamagaki''' (玉垣) refers to fences constructed around {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} or sacred areas ({{ill|shin'iki|ja|神域|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shin'iki|1=shin'iki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588853}}). It is also known as '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣).
== Overview ==
=== Origins and Evolution === As {{ill|Shrine Shinto|en|Shrine Shinto|WD=Q1527146|lt=Shinto shrine practices|1=Shrine Shinto}} became formalized and structures such as {{ill|shrine halls|ja|社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrine halls|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=社|WD=Q11588170}}, {{ill|Haiden (Shinto)|en|Haiden (Shinto)|WD=Q3125745|lt=haiden|1=Haiden (Shinto)}}, and {{ill|Honden|en|Honden|WD=Q2044994|lt=honden|1=Honden}} were established, the boundaries of shrine precincts became clearly demarcated. This distinction between the ambiguous boundary of the spiritual ({{ill|Tokoyo|ja|常世|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokoyo|1=Tokoyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q55521208}}) and the physical world ({{ill|Present life|en|Present life|WD=Q20183021|lt=Utsushiyo|1=Present life}}) evolved, transitioning from simple arrangements of sacred trees ({{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi|1=Himorogi}}) and rocks ({{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=iwa|1=Iwakura rock}} or {{ill|iwazakai|ja|磐境|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iwazakai|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座|1=Iwakura rock|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11587880}}) to more formal stone or wooden fences that define modern shrine layouts.
=== Types and Styles === The oldest form of tamagaki is thought to be {{ill|shibagaki|ja|柴垣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shibagaki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, fences made from entwined tree branches. Various styles have emerged, including: * '''Ita-tamagaki''': Fences made from thick wooden planks. * '''Kuroki-tamagaki''': Made from wood with the bark still attached. * '''Sukigaki''': Fences with widely spaced slats.
Materials vary from wood and stone to modern concrete. Tamagaki often bear inscriptions of the names of those who contributed them as offerings.
== Terminology == ; Synonyms Tamagaki has synonymous terms, including '''Mizugaki''' (瑞垣), '''Imigaki''' (斎垣), and '''Kamigaki''' (神垣), all meaning the same: a sacred fence. The inside of the fence is called "Kakitzu" (垣内), and the outside is "Kaito" (垣外). The terms "tama," "mizu," and "imi" signify sacred or beautiful, combining with "gaki" (fence) to mean "a beautiful sacred enclosure for the gods."
; Differentiation in multi-layered fences Shrines may have multiple layers of tamagaki, distinguished by specific names. The innermost fence is often called Mizugaki, while the outer layers are termed differently depending on the shrine, such as '''Itagaki''' (板垣) for the outermost fence or '''Aragaki''' (荒垣). For instance, at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, there are four concentric fences named, from innermost to outermost, Mizugaki, Uchitamagaki (内玉垣), Sototamagaki (外玉垣), and Itagaki.
== Ritual Equipment == As ritual equipment, miniature tamagaki are used to decorate {{ill|Kamidana|en|Kamidana|WD=Q327584|lt=kamidana|1=Kamidana}}, mimicking the fences seen at shrines.
== Notes == == See Also == * {{ill|Kannabi|en|Kannabi|WD=Q11588874|lt=Kannabi|1=Kannabi}} * {{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|WD=Q60587|lt=Himorogi|1=Himorogi}} * {{ill|Iwakura rock|en|Iwakura rock|WD=Q11587880|lt=Iwakura|1=Iwakura rock}} * {{ill|Chinju no Mori|en|Chinju no Mori|WD=Q11651158|lt=Chinju-no-mori|1=Chinju no Mori}}
